metadata.author: LivingPatch Contributors
metadata.description: Core LivingPatch dataset: Northeast Pennsylvania species, taxonomic groups, and ecological relationships

species.functional_description: Largest woodpecker in PA. Black with red crest. Excavates large rectangular holes in dead trees searching for carpenter ants, creating cavities used by owls, kestrels, and flying squirrels.
species.keystone_description: Primary cavity excavator. Abandoned cavities used by Great Horned Owl, American Kestrel, Wood Duck, and flying squirrels. Creates snags that structure the entire forest bird community.

species.functional_description: Large broad-winged hawk with rusty-red tail. Hunts from elevated perches and open soaring. Eats mostly small mammals; controls rodent and squirrel populations.
species.keystone_description: Apex daytime predator of open habitats. Controls gray squirrel, vole, and cottontail populations. Major fall migration corridor at Kittatinny Ridge (Hawk Mountain).

image.author: Becky Matsubara from El Sobrante, California

species.functional_description: Largest owl in PA. Earlike tufts, yellow eyes. Hunts at night. Earliest-nesting PA bird — eggs laid January–February. Uniquely tolerates skunk spray.
species.keystone_description: Apex nocturnal predator. Controls rabbits, squirrels, skunks, and even other raptors. Depends on Pileated Woodpecker cavities and abandoned Red-tailed Hawk nests.

species.functional_description: Sleek crested bird with yellow tail tip, red wax wing spots. One of PA's latest-nesting birds — timed to summer fruit peak. Highly frugivorous; major seed disperser of native shrubs.
Small warbler, male with black mask. Sings witchety-witchety-witch. Nests low in marsh vegetation. Host to Brown-headed Cowbird brood parasitism. Eats insects including butterflies.
Smallest falcon, robin-sized. Hovers over open fields. Eats grasshoppers in summer, voles year-round. Declining in PA due to farmland loss and Cooper's Hawk predation pressure. Uses nest boxes.
Small thrush with electric blue back, rusty breast. Cavity nester; uses nest boxes. Eats insects in summer, native berries in winter. Secondary cavity user — depends on woodpecker holes.
Smallest PA woodpecker. Black-and-white with small bill. Joins mixed winter flocks. Key winter forager: excavates goldenrod galls to eat Eurosta gall fly larvae inside — a critical food-web link.

Only breeding hummingbird in eastern NA. Arrives late April coinciding with Eastern Columbine bloom. Primary pollinator of red tubular native flowers. Also eats insects for protein; uses Yellow-bellied Sapsucker sap wells in spring.

Largest heron in NA — 4 ft tall. Stands motionless in shallows then strikes. Eats fish, frogs, snakes, rodents. Nests colonially in heronries of up to 500 nests. Strongly associated with beaver ponds in PA.
Largest NA rodent. Fells trees, builds dams, creates ponds. Transforms streams into wetland habitat that supports herons, ducks, frogs, and countless other species. Kits born April–June in lodge.
species.keystone_description: Single most powerful landscape engineer in NE PA. Beaver ponds create habitat for Great Blue Heron, Wood Duck, Hooded Merganser, American Bullfrog, Spotted Salamander, painted turtles, and muskrat. Beaver-killed snags multiply cavity supply for Pileated Woodpecker.

species.functional_description: Gray bushy-tailed rodent. Buries acorns in scatter-hoards, forgetting many — making it a critical oak regenerator alongside Blue Jays. Prefers White Oak acorns (lower tannins). Active year-round; no hibernation.

Most abundant large mammal in PA. Overabundant in absence of natural predators. Suppresses oak seedlings, eliminates native wildflowers (Trillium, orchids), and facilitates invasive plants. Fawns born (spotted) late May–June.
species.keystone_description: At high densities, suppresses oak seedling regeneration, eliminates native wildflowers (Trillium, native orchids), and facilitates invasive plant spread. When regulated by predators (coyote, bear), browsing pressure restores native understory diversity — a trophic cascade driver whose presence or absence reshapes the entire forest floor.

species.functional_description: Large tree with lobed leaves and rounded acorns. Preferred by wildlife over red oak — lower tannins make acorns palatable immediately on drop. Acorns germinate in fall. Supports 557 Lepidoptera species (genus Quercus). Lives 200+ years.
species.keystone_description: Hosts more wildlife than almost any other native tree. Acorns feed squirrels, deer, turkey, jays, woodpeckers, black bear. Leaves support 557 Lepidoptera caterpillar species. Long-lived — a single mature white oak feeds dozens of species.

species.functional_description: Large oak with bristle-tipped leaf lobes and red fall color. Acorns take 2 years to mature, ripen in fall and germinate the following spring — different from White Oak. Same 557-species Lepidoptera support as other native oaks.
species.keystone_description: Co-dominant with White Oak in NE PA upland forests. Acorns high-tannin — cached by squirrels longer than white oak acorns. Same broad wildlife support as Q. alba.

species.functional_description: Fast-growing tree of streambanks and floodplains. March–April catkins are critical early nectar/pollen for emerging bumble bee queens and Andrena bees. Primary food tree of Beaver. Roots stabilize eroding banks. Hosts 289+ Lepidoptera species.

Tall perennial with pink-purple globe flowers and milky toxic sap. Sole host for Monarch caterpillars (obligate at genus). Also hosts Red Milkweed Beetle, Large Milkweed Bug, and Milkweed Tussock Moth — all sequester its cardenolide toxins for self-defense.
species.keystone_description: Anchor of the milkweed guild. Monarch cannot breed without it. Also essential for Red Milkweed Beetle (root larvae obligate), Large Milkweed Bug (seed specialist), Milkweed Tussock Moth, and 11 additional Asclepias-specialist insects. Critical fall nectar source.

species.functional_description: Bright orange wildflower of dry, sandy soils. Unlike other milkweeds, has no milky sap. Host for Monarch caterpillars. Pollinated by butterflies, bumble bees, and hummingbirds. Seeds wind-dispersed via silky coma.

Pink-flowered milkweed of wet soils and stream edges. Host for Monarch caterpillars. Primary host for Swamp Milkweed Leaf Beetle. Blooms July–August, providing late summer nectar. Co-occurs with Joe-Pye weed, cardinal flower, blue vervain.

image.author: The original uploader was Teune at English Wikipedia.

species.functional_description: Yellow-flowered native perennials — PA has 15+ species. NOT the cause of hay fever (ragweed is). Blooms Aug–Oct, providing the most important late-season nectar in NE PA. Host for Eurosta gall fly (ball galls on stems, conspicuous in winter), whose larvae Downy Woodpeckers excavate. Supports 43 specialist bee species.
species.keystone_description: Dominant late-season nectar source for migrating Monarchs, 43 specialist native bee species, bumble bee gynes (fall queens building fat reserves), and dozens of generalist pollinators. Eurosta gall larvae are critical winter food for Downy Woodpeckers and Black-capped Chickadees.

species.functional_description: Red and yellow tubular flowers on rocky woodland slopes. Blooms late April–May — timed with Ruby-throated Hummingbird arrival. Primary pollinator is the hummingbird; bumble bees also access nectar. Host for Columbine Duskywing butterfly and Columbine Leafminer fly.

Fragrant understory shrub. Earliest woody bloom in PA forests (March–April), providing critical nectar before other plants open. Obligate host for Spicebush Swallowtail. Dioecious — only female plants produce the high-fat red berries that fuel fall migrating thrushes.

Medium tree — backbone of the Allegheny Plateau timber industry. Hosts 456 Lepidoptera species (genus Prunus). Small dark cherries in August eaten by 30+ bird species and many mammals. Primary PA host for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail and Eastern Tent Caterpillar.
species.keystone_description: Second only to oaks in total Lepidoptera support (456 species). Cherries fuel migrant thrushes, waxwings, and many other birds. Primary host for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail in PA. Eastern Tent Caterpillar — conspicuous spring indicator — depends on it.

species.functional_description: Orange and black butterfly toxic to most predators. Caterpillars obligate on genus Asclepias. 2–3 summer breeding generations in NE PA (June–September). Fall 'Methuselah' generation migrates 3,000+ miles to Mexico. Adults fuel on Goldenrod and asters during fall migration.

species.functional_description: Black butterfly with yellow spots, red eyespots on hindwing. Larvae obligate at Apiaceae family (carrot family): native Golden Alexanders, water hemlock, water parsnip; also Queen Anne's lace, dill, parsley. Young larvae mimic bird droppings. 2 broods in PA.

species.functional_description: Large yellow-and-black striped butterfly. Primary PA host is Black Cherry; also uses tulip tree, ash, willow, birch, and others — broad generalist. Late-instar caterpillars have large false eyespots mimicking a snake's head. Males gather at mud puddles. 2 broods in PA.

Caterpillars build silk-sealed leaf shelters on host plant (unfold curled leaves to find them). Late instars have spectacular false snake eyespots. Mimics toxic Pipevine Swallowtail as adult.

species.functional_description: Bright red-and-black longhorn beetle obligate on milkweed. Adults feed on foliage and flowers; uniquely, larvae develop in milkweed roots (hidden but critical). Sequesters cardenolide toxins — toxic and avoided by most birds. Adults active June–August on milkweed plants.

species.functional_description: Orange-and-black bug specialist on milkweed seeds. Sequesters cardenolide toxins — few predators. NE PA populations are migratory — cannot overwinter here; recolonize from southern states each summer. Nymphs form conspicuous red-and-black clusters on milkweed pods July–October.

species.functional_description: Fuzzy native bees — PA has 7+ resident species including Bombus impatiens (most common) and B. bimaculatus (earliest, late March). Only bumble bees can 'buzz pollinate' — vibrating to release pollen from blueberry, tomato, and other flowers. Queens overwinter in soil; workers and males die at frost. 43 specialist goldenrod bee species in Solidago genus.

species.keystone_description: Essential pollinators of milkweed, columbine, goldenrod, native blueberries, and 100+ other plants. Primary pollinators of Common Milkweed (produce 8x more seed pods than nocturnal moths). Buzz pollination is unique among bees. Black Willow catkins are critical first-of-spring food.

species.functional_description: Largest NA frog. Native to NE PA (invasive only in western US). Tadpoles take 1–2 winters to metamorphose — a rare multi-year aquatic life stage. Adults eat anything they can catch: insects, crayfish, fish, smaller frogs, small snakes. Deep jug-o-rum call carries long distances.

species.functional_description: Most colorful NA duck. Males iridescent green head, females brown with white teardrop eye-patch. Nests in cavities — depends on large cavities like those excavated by Pileated Woodpeckers or created by beavers. Perches on branches.

Nocturnal gliding squirrel. Stretches patagium (skin between limbs) to glide up to 150 feet. Nests in cavities created by Pileated Woodpeckers. Active at night hunting seeds, nuts, fungi, and insects.

image.author: (c) Louis Imbeau, some rights reserved (CC BY)

species.functional_description: Social caterpillars that build conspicuous silk tents in tree crotches during spring. Silk tents attract parasitoids and predatory wasps. Mass outbreaks occur cyclically (every 10-12 years). Important food source for birds.

Brilliant red male with black face mask; duller tan female. Thick conical bill adapted for cracking seeds. Year-round resident eating primarily seeds from grasses, forbs, and shrubs; supplements with insects during breeding season. Males sing year-round from exposed perches. Strong pair bond; both parents feed fledglings.

Striking blue, white, and black jay with prominent crest. Loud, harsh calls and excellent mimicry of hawk calls. Caches 3,000–5,000 acorns per fall — carrying up to 5 in its expandable throat pouch, transporting them up to 1 mile from source trees. In fragmented NE PA where forest patches are isolated by farms, roads, and development, blue jays are the primary mechanism connecting and regenerating oak stands. Also eats insects, eggs, and nestlings.
species.keystone_description: The keystone acorn disperser of eastern North America. Each Blue Jay caches 3,000–5,000 acorns per fall, carrying up to 5 in its expandable throat pouch and transporting them up to 1 mile — far beyond squirrel dispersal range. Responsible for rapid post-glacial oak reforestation. In today’s fragmented landscape, blue jays are the primary species moving oak seeds across farm fields, roads, and development gaps to establish new forest patches (Pesendorfer et al. 2016, Cornell Lab).

species.functional_description: Small, sleek, gray and white nuthatch that forages headfirst down tree trunks probing bark crevices for insects and spiders. Males have black caps; females gray. Acrobatic climber moving in all directions unlike other songbirds. Wedges sunflower seeds in bark crevices and hammers them open. Year-round resident that supplements winter diet with cached seeds.

Crested flycatcher with yellowish belly and russet tail. Hunts flying insects from exposed perches using aerial sallies — sallying up to 100 feet to catch flying insects in mid-air. Loud, sharp 'wheep' call. Nests in tree cavities, often using shed snake skin as lining material — function unknown but possibly deters predators. Migratory; arrives late April, departs by early September.

Common grasshoppers widespread in open habitats across PA. Short-horned locusts that jump using powerful hind legs. Males produce loud chirping songs by rubbing wings together to attract females. Important protein source for birds, reptiles, and small mammals. Nymphs and adults eat grasses, forbs, and crops. Peak abundance mid-summer through fall.

species.functional_description: Small bark beetles that tunnel through inner bark and sapwood of dead and dying pine, spruce, and fir trees. Adults and larvae create distinctive 'engraved' gallery patterns under bark. Important decomposers of dead wood, recycling nutrients back to soil. Attract predatory beetles and woodpeckers that excavate trees to hunt them. Each tunnel system represents multiple generations.

species.functional_description: Medium to large spiders that construct intricate geometric orb webs to trap flying insects. Webs rebuilt nightly at dusk; abandoned sections removed and eaten (protein recycling). Excellent hunters catching flies, midges, small moths, and other flying insects. Coloration camouflages them in webs during day. Favorite food of nuthatches, flycatchers, and wasps. Females lay egg sacs that overwinter in protected sites.

species.functional_description: Most common frog in NE PA ponds and stream edges. Males have bright green upper lip and prominent dorsolateral ridges — distinguishing it from Bullfrog. Territorial males produce banjo-twang calls from pond edges April–August. Bioindicator of water quality — absent from acidified or polluted water. Eats invertebrates, small fish, and other frogs; prey of herons, mink, snapping turtles, and large fish.

species.functional_description: Most abundant turtle in NE PA. Flat shell with red-orange markings on edges and yellow stripes on head and neck. Basks in rows on logs and rocks — conspicuous summer indicator of wetland health. Omnivorous: eats algae, aquatic plants, invertebrates, carrion, and small fish. Nests June–July in upland soil near water. Hatchlings may overwinter in nest, tolerating partial freezing. Strongly associated with beaver ponds.

species.functional_description: Common, aggressive dragonfly of NE PA ponds. Males vivid powder-blue; females bright green. Fiercely territorial — chases other dragonflies including much larger species. Adults eat mosquitoes, midges, and small flying insects. Aquatic nymphs (2–3 years) are top invertebrate predators in ponds, eating tadpoles, mosquito larvae, and small invertebrates. Both larval and adult stages provide significant pest control.

species.functional_description: Tall emergent wetland plant forming dense colonies in shallow water. Distinctive brown hot-dog seed heads visible year-round. Provides critical nesting cover for Red-winged Blackbird, Marsh Wren, and Common Yellowthroat. Rhizomes and seed fluff used by Muskrat and Beaver for food and lodge material. Each seed head releases up to 220,000 wind-dispersed seeds. Beaver pond creation dramatically expands cattail stands — the two species form a positive feedback loop.
species.keystone_description: Foundation species of NE PA freshwater marshes. Dense stands are obligate nesting habitat for marsh-breeding birds (Red-winged Blackbird, Marsh Wren, Common Yellowthroat). Root rhizomes are primary winter food for Muskrat. Beaver pond creation and cattail expansion are tightly linked — each amplifying the other.

image.author: Peter O'Connor aka anemoneprojectors from Stevenage, United Kingdom

species.functional_description: Large (7–9 in) black salamander with two rows of bright yellow spots. Lives almost entirely underground in forest floor. Emerges only on the first warm rainy nights of late February–March to breed in vernal pools — one of NE PA's most iconic wildlife events. Egg masses harbor symbiotic green algae (Oophila amblystomatis) that oxygenates the eggs. Adults return underground immediately after breeding; rarely seen otherwise. Eats earthworms, slugs, and soil invertebrates.

species.functional_description: Sleek, dark-brown semi-aquatic mustelid. Excellent swimmer; hunts along stream banks and pond edges. Eats frogs, fish, crayfish, muskrat, ducks, and small mammals. Dens in muskrat lodges and stream bank burrows. Year-round resident; active in winter under ice. Strongly associated with Beaver pond complexes and healthy stream corridors — presence indicates good water quality and prey base.

Medium to large deciduous tree with smooth gray bark and lance-shaped leaves. Produces edible triangular nuts (beechnuts) in hard spiny husks every 2-3 years (mast years). Critical food source for wildlife in fall and winter. Keystone species supporting diverse arthropod fauna. Long-lived (300+ years possible). Susceptible to beech bark disease and browsing pressure.

species.keystone_description: Keystone mast-producing tree supporting diverse wildlife in fall/winter including birds, mammals, and arthropods. Provides critical food when other resources scarce. Also key host for numerous insect species including aphids, scale insects, and defoliating insects. Long lifespan and structural complexity support diverse forest community.

species.functional_description: Small soft-bodied insects that form colonies on beech leaves and twigs, feeding on plant sap. Produce honeydew (sugary excretion) that attracts ants and other insects. Woolly-aphid forms have waxy white coating. High populations can reduce tree vigor but rarely cause severe damage. Aphids are primary food source for Harvester butterfly larvae through their relationship with attending ants.

species.functional_description: Unique butterfly — only North American butterfly species whose larvae feed on ants (specifically ants that tend aphid colonies). Small butterfly (wingspan 1.25-1.5 inches) with orange-brown wings. Adults feed on honeydew and nectar. Larvae hide among ant colonies and feed on worker ants while producing chemicals that mask them from ant detection. Critical predator controlling aphid populations through removal of tending ants.

species.functional_description: Medium-sized deciduous tree with large heart-shaped leaves and showy white trumpet flowers with purple spots and yellow throat. Flowers in spring attract pollinators and hummingbirds. Produces long thin seed pods (cigar-like) in fall. Native to southeastern US but naturalized in PA. Valuable as ornamental and host plant for Catalpa Sphinx larvae.

species.functional_description: Large sphinx moth (hawk moth) with wingspans 3-3.5 inches. Adults are nocturnal and attracted to lights. Gray-brown coloration provides camouflage on tree bark. Larvae (caterpillars) are distinctive yellow-green with dark bands and two tentacle-like filaments on head. Obligate host plant is Catalpa. Larvae can defoliate trees but rarely cause serious damage. Known locally as 'catalpa worms' — used as fish bait.

species.functional_description: Large sphinx moth similar to Catalpa Sphinx but with more wavy lines on wings (hence the name). Wingspan 3-3.5 inches. Adults are nocturnal. Host plants include Fringe tree and occasionally other plants. Less specifically tied to one host compared to Catalpa Sphinx. Important part of nocturnal insect community.

species.functional_description: Medium-sized flycatcher (8-9 inches) with distinctive black and white plumage and concealed red crown patch (rarely visible). Aggressive and highly territorial — known for chasing larger birds including hawks and crows from their territory. Expert aerial insectivore constantly sallying out to catch insects in flight. Nests in open branches where nests are highly visible. Harsh, buzzing calls are characteristic of open field habitat.

species.functional_description: Small iridescent blue-green swallow with white underparts. Highly aerial insectivore — constantly in flight hawking small insects and flies. Uses cavities in dead trees and nest boxes. Highly colonial; often nests in small clusters. Important indicator of water quality and healthy insect populations in open habitats.

life_stages.name: Fledgling
life_stages.description: Newly fledged young learning to hawk insects in flight

life_stages.name: Pre-migration
life_stages.description: Feeding intensively on flying insects before southbound migration

species.keystone_description: Critical aerial insectivore in open habitats and water margins. Single pair can consume thousands of mosquitoes and flies per season. Population health reflects water quality and insect abundance. Requires cavity nesting sites (dead trees or boxes) and open foraging habitat in proximity.

species.functional_description: Sleek blue-black and rust-colored swallow with deeply forked tail. Builds mud nests on barn eaves, bridge structures, and buildings. Skims low over water and open ground catching insects. Highly vocal and social. Often feeds in mixed-species flocks with other swallows.

life_stages.name: Nest Building
life_stages.description: Pairs build mud cup nests on barn eaves and structures

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Clutch of 3-5 white eggs with rusty spots

life_stages.name: Chick
life_stages.description: Nestlings in mud nest; parents constantly bring insects

life_stages.name: Fledgling
life_stages.description: Young swallows still being fed by parents while learning to hunt

species.functional_description: Brilliant orange-and-black male; olive-yellow female. Builds a distinctive woven pendant nest 4–7 inches deep hanging from branch tips over open areas or water — often in black willow, sycamore, or elm. One of few songbirds that eats hairy caterpillars (tent caterpillars, fall webworm) that other birds avoid due to chemical defenses; a key natural check on spring caterpillar outbreaks. Pollinates tubular flowers when nectaring. Eats ripe cherries, elderberries, and mulberries in mid-summer. Arrives early May; departs by early September for neotropical wintering grounds in Central America.

life_stages.name: Territory Establishment
life_stages.description: Males arrive and begin defending territory with song from canopy perches

life_stages.name: Nest Building
life_stages.description: Female weaves intricate pendant nest hanging from branch tips, often over water

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Clutch of 3-6 pale gray eggs with dark irregular markings

life_stages.name: Chick
life_stages.description: Nestlings raised in pendant nest; parents deliver caterpillars and insects

life_stages.name: Fledgling
life_stages.description: Young orioles follow parents while learning to forage; diet shifts to fruit and nectar

life_stages.name: Pre-migration
life_stages.description: Building fat reserves on fruit and insects before southbound migration to Central America

species.functional_description: Smallest North American oriole. Adult males have rich chestnut body with black hood and back; first-year males are olive-yellow with a black bib; females olive-yellow throughout. More nectarivorous than Baltimore Oriole — probes a wider variety of flowers and is a consistent pollinator in open habitats. Arrives late May (1–2 weeks after Baltimore), but departs by early August — the shortest summer residency of any NE PA breeder. Nests in orchards, shrubby riparian edges, and open forest margins. Caterpillars and other insects dominate spring diet; shifts to nectar and soft fruit through summer.

life_stages.name: Arrival
life_stages.description: Males arrive and quickly establish territories in open woodland edges and orchards

life_stages.name: Nest Building
life_stages.description: Female builds compact woven cup nest, shallower than Baltimore Oriole's deep pendant

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Clutch of 3-5 pale blue eggs with brown markings

life_stages.name: Chick
life_stages.description: Nestlings fed insects and caterpillars; rapid growth and early fledging

life_stages.name: Early Departure
life_stages.description: Adults and fledglings begin southbound migration — earliest of NE PA's summer residents

species.functional_description: Dominant conifer of cool ravines and north-facing slopes in NE PA. Dense year-round canopy shades trout streams 10–15°F cooler than open reaches — critical for wild brook and brown trout. Provides thermal refuge for White-tailed Deer and Elk during severe winters. Supports 90+ Lepidoptera species. Now severely threatened by Hemlock Woolly Adelgid (Adelges tsugae), an invasive aphid-like insect spreading northward; PA populations increasingly stressed or dead in southern portions of range. Losing hemlocks from ravines causes stream temperature spikes that extirpate native trout populations.
species.keystone_description: Creates unique cool-stream and ravine microhabitats that no other PA tree can replicate. Hemlock groves are thermal refugia for deer and elk in winter, support brook trout via stream cooling, and provide cavity substrate for Pileated Woodpecker. Loss of hemlock causes cascading collapse of cool-stream ecosystems.

species.functional_description: North America's second-largest deer species. Extirpated from PA by 1877; successfully reintroduced in Elk County (north-central PA) starting 1913. Current PA population ~1,500 animals centered on Clearfield, Cameron, Clinton, and Elk counties — edges of NE PA range. Bulls carry massive antlers shed each spring; rut occurs September–October with bugling calls. Elk graze meadows and browse forest edges, heavily cropping grasses, sedges, and forbs. Winter herds congregate in open fields near Benezette. Calves born May–June; vulnerable to coyote and black bear predation for first weeks of life.

Largest land predator in PA. Black bears are omnivores with diet shifting seasonally: spring brings carrion, emerging vegetation, and insects (especially ant colonies and grubs); summer focuses on berries and soft mast; fall hyperphagia drives heavy acorn, beechnut, and wild cherry consumption to build fat for winter. Dens November–March (females with cubs remain dormant longer). Cubs born January–February in den; 1–4 per litter. Sow defends cubs aggressively. Important predator of White-tailed Deer fawns in May–June and Elk calves. PA black bear population ~20,000; one of the highest densities in the eastern US.

Medium-sized masked omnivore abundant throughout NE PA. Highly adaptable; thrives in forests, wetlands, and suburban edges. Diet includes crayfish, frogs, turtle eggs, salamander egg masses, bird eggs, invertebrates, fruit, acorns, and carrion. Den in tree cavities (especially Pileated Woodpecker excavations), hollow logs, and groundhog burrows. Litters of 2–5 kits born April–May. Major predator of wetland amphibian eggs (spotted salamander, wood frog) and painted turtle nests. Raccoon density in NE PA has increased substantially with forest fragmentation and suburban expansion.

The Eastern Coyote is a hybrid of western Coyote and Eastern Wolf, larger than its western counterpart (35–55 lbs). Colonized PA by the 1980s following wolf extirpation. Now established throughout NE PA. Primary predators of White-tailed Deer fawns (May–June) and weakened adults in winter, providing a partial top-down check on deer populations in the absence of wolves. Also takes Elk calves, rabbits, groundhogs, raccoons, and carrion. Pair-bonds year-round; pups born April–May in earthen dens. Howling choruses audible year-round. Suppresses Red Fox through direct competition — foxes retreat to more suburban edges where coyote pressure is lower.

PA's largest ground squirrel. Excavates extensive burrow systems (5+ ft deep, 25+ ft long) with multiple entrances — creating shelter used by Virginia Opossum, Raccoon, Red Fox, Cottontail Rabbit, and numerous snake and invertebrate species long after the groundhog vacates or dies. True hibernator: enters torpor October–November and emerges February–March. Primarily a grazer and browser of grasses, clovers, and garden crops. 2–6 young born April–May underground. Major prey base for Red-tailed Hawk, Eastern Coyote, Red Fox, and Black Bear throughout the active season.

North America's only marsupial; young complete development in mother's pouch. Diet is extremely broad: carrion, invertebrates, fruit, bird eggs, ticks (a single opossum consumes 5,000+ ticks per season), frogs, snakes, and garbage. Highly resistant to rabies due to lower body temperature. Immune to pit viper venom. Famously 'plays dead' (thanatosis) when threatened. Den in hollow logs, brush piles, and abandoned groundhog or skunk burrows. Litters of 6–20 joeys born after 13-day gestation; 8–9 survive on average attached to nipples in pouch for 2 months. Expanded northward in PA with warming winters over past 40 years.

A holoparasitic plant with no chlorophyll — steals carbohydrates entirely from the roots of oaks (Quercus spp.). Emerges as pale yellow-brown, corn-cob-like clusters from the forest floor in April–June. Cannot photosynthesize; its entire carbon budget comes from host root connections that develop as a dense underground tuber-like mass. One of the most important early-spring food sources for American Black Bear: bears emerge from dens in late March–April when calorie-rich foods are scarce and actively seek bear corn patches, which recur in the same locations year to year. The plant's common name derives directly from this relationship.

Rusty-red medium canid with white-tipped tail. Highly adaptable generalist predator of forest edges and open habitats. Diet includes voles, meadow mice, cottontail rabbits, groundhogs, birds, insects, berries, and carrion. Uses a distinctive pouncing 'mousing' technique to catch prey under snow. Monogamous pairs den in earthen burrows February–May; 4–6 kits per litter. Eastern Coyote is a dominant competitor — coyote colonization of NE PA since the 1980s has displaced Red Fox from core forest habitats into suburban fringes where coyote pressure is reduced. Red Fox in turn suppresses populations of smaller predators like weasels.

Most common bat in PA and historically the most abundant in NE PA. Tiny (7–14 g) with copper-brown fur and black ears. Can eat 600–1,000 insects per hour using echolocation, consuming mosquitoes, midges, moths, and beetles in continuous feeding flights over water and forest edges. Severely impacted by White-nose Syndrome (Pseudogymnoascus destructans), a fungal pathogen that disrupts hibernation torpor — NE PA populations have declined 90%+ since the syndrome arrived in 2007. Summer maternity colonies of 20–300 females roost in warm buildings, hollow trees, and under bark; males roost solitarily. Hibernates October–April in caves and mines. Gives birth to a single pup in June.

life_stages.name: Maternity Colony
life_stages.description: Females form warm roost colonies in buildings or hollow trees to give birth

life_stages.name: Pup Birth
life_stages.description: Single pup born; carried by mother initially, then left at roost while she forages

life_stages.name: Pup Fledging
life_stages.description: Young bats learn to fly and echolocate; begin independent foraging

life_stages.name: Pre-hibernation Fattening
life_stages.description: Intensive foraging to build fat reserves before hibernation; weight nearly doubles

life_stages.name: Hibernation
life_stages.description: Overwinters in caves and mines in dense clusters; vulnerable to White-nose Syndrome during this period

species.keystone_description: Critical nighttime insect suppressor. A single bat consumes 600–1,000 insects per hour; a maternity colony of 150 bats can eat 1.3 million insects per night. White-nose Syndrome has eliminated 90%+ of PA's Little Brown Bat populations since 2007 — the resulting insect release is measurable in agricultural pest damage data. Their loss represents one of the most severe ecological disruptions in recent PA natural history.

species.functional_description: Larger and more robust than Little Brown Bat (14–23 g). More resistant to White-nose Syndrome than most Myotis bats — PA populations have declined less severely, and Big Brown Bats are increasingly the dominant bat species in NE PA as WNS continues to devastate Little Brown Bat colonies. Ecologically distinct from Little Brown Bat: Big Browns specialize in hard-bodied beetles (including agricultural pest species like cucumber beetles and June bugs) rather than mosquitoes. A single colony can consume 33 million rootworm beetles per summer season. Frequently roosts in building attics and wall voids year-round; also uses tree cavities and rock crevices.

life_stages.name: Maternity Colony
life_stages.description: Females roost in buildings or tree cavities; smaller colonies than Little Brown Bat

life_stages.name: Pup Birth
life_stages.description: 1–2 pups born; among the few bat species regularly having twins

life_stages.name: Pup Fledging
life_stages.description: Young bats begin foraging; diet immediately focuses on beetles

life_stages.name: Pre-hibernation Fattening
life_stages.description: Intensive beetle hunting to build fat before overwintering

life_stages.name: Hibernation
life_stages.description: Overwinters in caves, mines, and building voids; greater WNS survival than most PA bats

species.functional_description: One of the most ecologically critical native wildflowers in NE PA. Tall (2–6 ft) with deep purple ray flowers and yellow disk centers. Blooms September–October — among the very last native plants to flower before frost, providing an irreplaceable nectar bridge for migrating Monarchs, native bumble bees building winter fat reserves, and dozens of butterfly and beetle species. A single aster plant can produce thousands of florets. Colonizes roadsides, wet meadows, and disturbed edges; thrives in full sun to light shade.
species.keystone_description: The keystone late-season wildflower of NE PA. Blooms when virtually all other native nectar sources have gone to seed — providing critical late-migration fuel for Monarch Butterfly (fall migration) and the last major pollen and nectar resource for native bumble bee queens before winter dormancy. Loss of roadside and meadow aster patches is linked to bumble bee colony failure rates in fall.

species.functional_description: Tall native wildflower (2–4 ft) with showy white-to-pale-pink tubular flowers that closely resemble true foxglove (Digitalis). One of the most common native Penstemon in eastern North America. Blooms May–July in open meadows, forest edges, and roadsides. Tubular flowers with a hairy lower lip ('beardtongue') are precisely shaped for bumble bee pollination — large bees force entry and contact anthers on their backs. Also visited by Ruby-throated Hummingbirds probing for nectar. Long-lived perennial forming attractive clumps. Critical bridge-season pollinator plant between spring ephemerals and summer wildflowers.

Small fast-growing tree (15–35 ft) that is the first woody plant to colonize clear-cuts, burns, and forest disturbances in NE PA. Named for its tiny bright-red cherries borne in clusters along the branches in July–August. Among the first trees to bloom in spring (April–May), providing critical early nectar when bumble bee queens are newly emerged. Supports a rich caterpillar community on Prunus foliage. Tiny cherries are eagerly eaten by Cedar Waxwings, American Robins, thrushes, and Black Bears — making Pin Cherry one of the most important wildlife food trees in early successional habitat.

Large native shrub or small tree (up to 30 ft) bearing dense clusters of dark red-to-black astringent cherries in late summer. One of the most important wildlife food shrubs in NE PA: the fruits are heavily used by Cedar Waxwings, American Robins, thrushes, Black Bear, and other frugivores from August through September. Blooms in May with showy white cylindrical flower clusters that attract bumble bees and other pollinators. Major larval host for Eastern Tent Caterpillar — silk tents in branch forks are a classic spring sight. The name 'chokecherry' reflects the fruit's intense astringency when raw; the same compounds (cyanogenic glycosides) deter most mammal herbivores but don't stop birds.

taxonomic_groups.label: Herons & Egrets
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Wading birds; stalk and strike prey in shallow water; colonial nesters in heronries

taxonomic_groups.label: Woodpeckers
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Bark foragers; excavate wood for food and nesting; primary and secondary cavity creators

taxonomic_groups.label: Oaks
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Mast-producing trees; support 557+ Lepidoptera species; foundational forest structure

taxonomic_groups.label: Milkweeds
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Cardenolide-producing host plants for Monarch and milkweed guild specialists; nectar sources

taxonomic_groups.label: Willows
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Early-blooming riparian trees and shrubs; beaver food source; support 289+ Lepidoptera species

taxonomic_groups.label: Frogs
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Aquatic breeders; bioindicators of water quality; chorus callers in spring; pond and wetland predators
taxonomic_groups.notes: Order Anura; families Ranidae and Hylidae

taxonomic_groups.label: Carrot-Family Plants
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Flat-topped flower clusters (umbels); obligate hosts for Black Swallowtail larvae at family level

taxonomic_groups.label: Native Fruiting Shrubs
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Berry-producing native shrubs; bird-dispersed seeds; critical fall and winter food for frugivores
taxonomic_groups.notes: Various families; includes Amelanchier, Cornus, Viburnum, Sambucus, Ilex, Vaccinium

taxonomic_groups.label: Songbirds
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Perching birds with complex vocalizations; diverse diets; important seed dispersers and insect controllers
taxonomic_groups.notes: Order Passeriformes; includes Cardinals, Jays, Nuthatches, Flycatchers, and other perching birds

taxonomic_groups.label: Deer Family (Cervids)
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Large hoofed mammals; males bear antlers shed annually; browsers and grazers; shape forest understory through herbivory
taxonomic_groups.notes: Family Cervidae; includes White-tailed Deer and Elk in NE PA

taxonomic_groups.label: Canids
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Dog-family predators; territorial; pair-bonded; opportunistic hunters controlling mesopredator and herbivore populations
taxonomic_groups.notes: Family Canidae; Eastern Coyote (hybrid wolf-coyote) and Red Fox in NE PA; compete for overlapping territories with coyote dominant

taxonomic_groups.label: Bats
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Nocturnal flying mammals; echolocation hunters; primary suppressors of night-flying insects; cave and mine hibernators; severely threatened by White-nose Syndrome
taxonomic_groups.notes: Order Chiroptera; Little Brown Bat and Big Brown Bat are the most common species in NE PA; 90%+ population decline since White-nose Syndrome arrived in 2007

taxonomic_groups.label: Wild Cherries & Chokecherry
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Native Prunus trees and shrubs; prolific fruit producers fueling frugivore migration; primary hosts for Eastern Tent Caterpillar and 456+ Lepidoptera species; early spring bloomers for pollinators
taxonomic_groups.notes: Genus Prunus (family Rosaceae); Black Cherry, Pin Cherry, and Chokecherry are the key NE PA representatives

symbiosis.notes: Monarch caterpillars obligate on genus Asclepias. A. syriaca is preferred oviposition plant in NE PA due to moderate cardenolide levels.
Monarchs nectar heavily on goldenrod during fall migration — critical fat-building fuel before Mexico journey.
Red Milkweed Beetle obligate on genus Asclepias. Adults on foliage/flowers; larvae develop in roots — unique in Cerambycidae.
T. melanurus (tuberosa specialist) and occasionally T. tetrophthalmus use Butterfly Weed.
Large Milkweed Bug specialist on Asclepias seeds. Also feeds on pods, flowers, and vegetative tissue. Sequesters cardenolides.
Feeds on Butterfly Weed seeds and pods.
Feeds on Swamp Milkweed seeds.
Bumble bees are primary diurnal pollinators of Common Milkweed — produce 8x more mature pods than nocturnal moth visitors per Fritz & Morse 1981.
Bumble bees and butterflies pollinate Butterfly Weed, which lacks milky sap barrier.
B. impatiens and B. griseocollis are primary pollinators of Swamp Milkweed.
Goldenrod is critical fall pollen/nectar source for bumble bee gynes building fat reserves for winter. 43 Solidago specialist bee species.
Long-tongued bumble bee workers reach nectar in columbine spurs. Short-tongued bees rob through spur holes.
Black Willow catkins (March–April) are critical first-of-spring pollen and nectar for emerging bumble bee queens before other plants open.
Bumble bees are primary pollinators of Spicebush early spring flowers alongside Andrena bees and small flies.

Ruby-throated Hummingbird is primary pollinator of Eastern Columbine — deposits 10x more pollen per visit than bees per University of Maryland Extension. Bloom timing co-evolved with hummingbird spring arrival.
Spicebush Swallowtail obligate at Lauraceae family. Primary PA hosts are Spicebush and Sassafras. Larvae build silk-sealed folded-leaf shelters.
Black Cherry is the primary PA host plant for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail caterpillars.
White Oak is a facultative host for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail — used alongside many other woody plants.
Red Oak is a facultative host for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail.
Black Willow is a facultative host for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail and supports several other swallowtail species.
Black Swallowtail is obligate at family Apiaceae. Uses native Golden Alexanders, water hemlock, water parsnip, and naturalized/cultivated Queen Anne's lace, parsley, dill, fennel.
Black Willow is a primary food tree for Beaver — bark and cambium eaten; tree felled for dam/lodge construction.
Beaver uses all native willow species for food and dam construction material.
Beaver ponds and adjacent wetlands are primary foraging habitat for Great Blue Heron in NE PA. Beaver activity creates the shallow productive water herons depend on.
Great Blue Heron is a primary predator of American Bullfrog adults and large tadpoles.
Pileated Woodpecker excavates cavities used by Great Horned Owl for nesting.
American Kestrel uses abandoned Pileated Woodpecker cavities and Northern Flicker holes for nesting; also uses nest boxes.
Great Horned Owl is the primary natural predator of Red-tailed Hawk — takes eggs, nestlings, and occasionally adults.
Eastern Gray Squirrel is a major prey item for Red-tailed Hawk in forested PA landscapes.
Eastern Gray Squirrel is a significant prey item for Great Horned Owl, especially in winter.
Great Horned Owl takes large frogs including Bullfrog adults near beaver ponds and wetlands.
Gray Squirrel preferentially caches and eats White Oak acorns (lower tannins, less bitter than red oak). A critical oak-regeneration mutualism — forgotten caches germinate into new trees.
Gray Squirrel caches Red Oak acorns — nips embryo tip before burial (preventing germination) then retrieves them. Red Oak acorns cached longer than white oak.
Gray Squirrel uses all native oak species — primary mast consumer and seed disperser across all Quercus.
White-tailed Deer eats White Oak acorns intensively in fall, and browses seedlings year-round — overabundant deer suppresses oak regeneration.

Deer browse Spicebush stems and leaves — heavy deer pressure can suppress Spicebush in understory, eliminating the obligate larval host for Spicebush Swallowtail. Where deer density is high, Spicebush is consistently browsed below the browse line, collapsing available oviposition sites and reducing Spicebush Swallowtail abundance in affected forest stands.
Black Cherry is a major Cedar Waxwing food source — flocks strip cherries in August. Waxwings pass seeds through gut intact, dispersing them widely.
High-fat Spicebush berries (female plants only) are critical fall food for Cedar Waxwings and migrant thrushes.
Cedar Waxwing is a generalist frugivore of all native berry-producing shrubs — serviceberry, viburnum, dogwood, elderberry, winterberry. Major seed disperser.
Eastern Bluebird eats Black Cherry fruit in late summer and fall.
Bluebirds shift to native berries (viburnum, sumac, bayberry, holly) in winter when insects unavailable.
American Kestrel occasionally preys on Eastern Bluebird, especially where both use open-country nest boxes.
Downy Woodpecker excavates Eurosta gall fly ball-galls on goldenrod stems in winter to eat larvae inside — a critical food-web link between goldenrod and forest birds.
Common Yellowthroat catches butterflies and other flying insects including occasional Monarch adults.
All native PA Asclepias species — Poke Milkweed (forest shade), Whorled Milkweed (dry open), Four-leaved (woodland) — support Monarch larvae.
Eastern Tiger Swallowtail uses multiple oak species as facultative larval hosts beyond White and Red Oak.
Wood Duck nests in cavities excavated by Pileated Woodpecker. Woodpecker provides critical cavity homes in dead trees where wood ducks depend for successful breeding.

Northern Flying Squirrels nest in Pileated Woodpecker cavities. These large cavities (1.5 inches diameter) are critical winter shelter for flying squirrels — key to their survival in northern PA winters.
Black Cherry is the primary PA host for Eastern Tent Caterpillar. Silk tents in spring branches are conspicuous ecosystem indicator. Mass outbreaks attract numerous predators and parasitoids.
Black Willow is a facultative host for Spicebush Swallowtail caterpillars alongside primary Lauraceae family hosts (Spicebush, Sassafras). Larvae build silk-sealed folded-leaf shelters.

Blue Jay is the primary long-distance disperser of White Oak acorns in NE PA. Carries up to 5 acorns in expandable throat pouch; transports them up to 1 mile from source trees. Forgotten caches germinate into new trees, regenerating oak stands across fragmented landscape (Pesendorfer et al. 2016).

Blue Jay caches Red Oak acorns over longer distances than squirrels. High-tannin acorns are stored deeper and longer — increasing germination probability. Blue Jays select canopy gaps and forest edges as cache sites, preferentially restoring fragmented stands.

Blue Jays are the single most important long-distance oak disperser in eastern NA — responsible for rapid post-glacial oak reforestation and ongoing regeneration of isolated forest patches in today's fragmented landscape. Dispersal distances (up to 1+ mile) far exceed any other acorn-caching animal.
Great Blue Heron is a primary predator of Green Frog adults along pond and stream edges.
Great Blue Heron occasionally takes juvenile Painted Turtles; adults too large to handle.
Mink is a significant predator of American Bullfrog along beaver ponds and stream edges; hunts at night along shorelines.
Mink preys on Wood Duck eggs and ducklings; a natural predation pressure on cavity-nesting ducks in beaver pond habitat.
Green Frog is a common prey item for Mink along pond margins and stream banks.

Beaver pond creation floods low-lying areas, dramatically expanding the shallow standing water that Common Cattail requires. Cattail colonies in turn stabilize pond edges and provide lodge building material for Beaver — a positive feedback loop driving wetland expansion.

Adult Eastern Pondhawks are prey for Green Frogs and American Bullfrogs along pond edges. Pondhawk nymphs in turn eat small tadpoles and aquatic invertebrates — a reverse predation dynamic across life stages.
Common Cattail stands bordering vernal pools provide overwintering cover and humid terrestrial microhabitat for Spotted Salamanders migrating between forest and breeding pools.
Beech aphids feed on beech tree sap, causing minor damage. Mostly kept in check by natural enemies and weather. Honeydew production attracts ants.

Harvester butterfly larvae feed on ants that tend beech aphids. By removing protective ant attendants, larvae indirectly control aphid populations and disrupt ant-aphid mutualism. Critical regulation of aphid colonies.
Catalpa Sphinx Moth larvae are obligate on Southern Catalpa. Caterpillars feed on foliage; can cause significant defoliation in heavy infestations but tree usually recovers.
Both species forage in same areas and feed on flying insects. Often seen in mixed-species feeding flocks during migration. Different nesting substrate preferences minimize direct competition.
Northern Cardinal eats Black Cherry fruit in late summer. Males and females both take ripe cherries — a consistent consumer whose seeds pass through gut intact.
Red-tailed Hawk occasionally takes Northern Cardinal at forest edges and near brush piles — an opportunistic prey item rather than a dietary staple.

White-breasted Nuthatch caches White Oak acorns in bark crevices and ground soil for winter use. Uncached acorns germinate — nuthatch is a secondary oak disperser alongside Blue Jay, operating at shorter range within its territory.

White-breasted Nuthatch uses smaller abandoned cavities created by Pileated Woodpecker and Northern Flicker for roosting and winter insulation. Also forages in bark furrows opened by woodpecker excavation.
Great Crested Flycatcher is an obligate cavity nester — preferentially uses large abandoned Pileated Woodpecker cavities and Northern Flicker holes in NE PA. Famously lines nest with shed snakeskin.
Great Crested Flycatcher takes large caterpillars including Eastern Tent Caterpillar during spring outbreaks — sallies from perch to grab crawling caterpillars from branches.

Field Grasshopper is the primary summer prey for American Kestrel in NE PA open habitats — grasshoppers can comprise 50%+ of summer diet. Kestrel hovers over meadows specifically targeting grasshoppers.
Eastern Bluebird hunts grasshoppers and crickets in open fields, dropping from low perches onto prey. Grasshoppers are critical summer protein especially for nestlings.

Engraver Bark Beetles (Ips spp.) colonize stressed, dying, or recently-dead Black Cherry trees. Larvae engrave characteristic gallery patterns under bark, girdling cambium and accelerating tree death. Mass outbreaks follow drought or ice damage.

Bark beetles and carpenter ants are the two primary prey driving Pileated Woodpecker foraging. Pileated excavates large rectangular holes specifically to reach bark beetle larval galleries in dead and dying trees — the foundational predation link explaining why Pileated Woodpeckers are drawn to snags.

Downy Woodpecker forages on smaller bark beetle galleries near the bark surface — especially on smaller-diameter branches where Pileated cannot reach. Downy and Pileated partition the bark beetle resource by tree diameter and depth.

Common Orb-weaver Spider builds large orb webs (up to 60 cm) in meadow and forest-edge vegetation where Monarchs and bumble bees forage. Spiders opportunistically capture both species — though Monarchs' cardenolide load sometimes causes spiders to release them.

Common Yellowthroat actively gleans spiders from vegetation and low shrubs — spiders are a consistent dietary item. Orb-weaver spiders in meadow edge habitat are frequently taken during the Yellowthroat's low foraging passes.

Ceratomia undulosa (Waved Sphinx Moth) uses White Oak as a primary larval host in NE PA. Caterpillars feed on oak foliage from June–August. Related Sphingidae also use Ash and Fringe Tree but White Oak is the dominant PA host.

Eastern Kingbird is an aerial predator of large moths including hawkmoths (Sphingidae). Waved Sphinx Moths fly at dusk along forest edges — exactly the habitat where Kingbirds make late-evening aerial sallies.

Eastern Kingbird's original name was 'Bee Martin' — it regularly captures large bees and wasps in aerial sallies. Bumble bees are taken along meadow edges and near beaver ponds. Kingbirds remove stingers by rubbing prey against a perch before swallowing.

Eastern Kingbird forages over open water and beaver pond edges where dragonflies concentrate. Eastern Pondhawk is a conspicuous mid-sized dragonfly in the same open habitats — a regular prey item for aerial sallying kingbirds.

Beaver pond creation transforms stream channels into the still, shallow, productive water that American Bullfrog and Green Frog require for breeding and year-round residence. A single active beaver colony supports dozens of adult frogs and thousands of tadpoles annually.

American Bullfrog is an opportunistic predator in shared wetland habitat — takes Spotted Salamander adults migrating to breeding pools, and consumes egg masses and larvae when they overlap with bullfrog foraging range.

Blue Jay preys on Eastern Tent Caterpillar larvae during mass outbreak years. Silk tent colonies on Black Cherry attract conspicuous predation — Blue Jays tear open tents and take caterpillars directly.

White-tailed Deer congregate in hemlock groves during winter storms and extreme cold — the dense evergreen canopy reduces wind chill and snow depth by 50–70%, dramatically cutting energy expenditure. Hemlock yards are critical survival habitat during severe NE PA winters.
Elk use hemlock stands as thermal cover during winter. Dense hemlock groves buffer wind and retain heat, reducing caloric costs for elk wintering in NE PA's northern ranges.

Elk consume acorns heavily during fall hyperphagia, competing with White-tailed Deer, Black Bear, turkeys, and squirrels for the same mast resource. Bull elk also strip bark from oak saplings during velvet rub in late summer, killing young trees.

Elk and White-tailed Deer compete for browse, grasses, and mast where ranges overlap at the edge of the PA elk range. Elk are dominant due to size; deer shift diet and space use in areas of high elk density.

Eastern Coyote targets newborn Elk calves (May–June) when calves are left alone in vegetation by the cow. Coyote predation accounts for significant calf mortality in NE PA during the first weeks of life.
Black Bear is an important predator of Elk calves in late May–June. Bears locate and consume calves hidden in vegetation; a primary mortality source for neonatal elk in PA's recovered population.

Black Bear is a significant predator of White-tailed Deer fawns during May–June when spotted fawns are left alone by does. Fawn predation by bears can account for 15–30% of fawn mortality in NE PA's forested landscapes.

Black Bear consumes massive quantities of acorns during fall hyperphagia (August–November), gaining 1–3 lbs per day in preparation for denning. A single bear may consume 20,000 calories per day from mast alone. White Oak acorns are preferred due to lower tannins.
Black Bear is a primary consumer and disperser of Black Cherry fruit in late summer. Bears climb trees, break branches, and consume fruit in quantity — dispersing seeds widely through scat.

Beechnuts are a critical high-fat mast for Black Bear in NE PA. Bears climb mature beeches and eat nuts directly, leaving distinctive claw marks. Beechnut failure years drive bears into suburban areas seeking food.
Black Bear and Eastern Coyote compete for deer and elk fawns as prey, carrion, and berry resources. Bears are dominant — coyotes typically yield to approaching bears at carcass sites.

Raccoon is a primary predator of Spotted Salamander egg masses and migrating adults during the brief February–March breeding window. Raccoons learn vernal pool locations and concentrate there during migration nights, consuming eggs and adults in quantity — a major source of salamander mortality.
Raccoon preys on frogs year-round wherever accessible, especially in shallow beaver ponds and stream edges. Washes prey in water before eating — characteristic foraging behavior at wetland margins.

Raccoon is the primary predator of Painted Turtle nest sites. Females excavate nests in June–July and raccoons locate and excavate 50–90% of nests within days of laying in many NE PA sites. A leading cause of turtle population decline in human-adjacent wetlands.
Raccoon dens in tree cavities excavated by Pileated Woodpecker. Large pileated cavities in older trees are preferred den sites, especially for nursing females in spring.
Raccoon uses abandoned Groundhog burrows as temporary dens, particularly in winter when seeking shelter below the frost line.

Eastern Coyote is the primary large predator of White-tailed Deer in PA in the absence of wolves. Targets fawns (May–June) and weakened/injured adults in winter. Coyote predation provides the strongest remaining top-down check on deer populations in NE PA, helping restore native understory vegetation where coyotes are present.
Groundhog is a major prey item for Eastern Coyote in field and edge habitats throughout spring and summer. Coyotes hunt them by stalking through meadows and rushing burrow openings.
Eastern Coyote occasionally preys on Raccoon, particularly juveniles in summer and adults during winter when food is scarce.

Eastern Coyote is the dominant competitor of Red Fox in NE PA. Coyotes actively displace foxes from core forest and meadow territories through direct aggression and territorial exclusion — a process called intraguild predation. Fox populations have retreated to suburban and human-edge habitats where coyote pressure is reduced.
Eastern Coyote takes Eastern Gray Squirrels opportunistically, especially juveniles on the ground in late summer when dispersing from nests.

Groundhog is one of the largest prey items regularly taken by Red-tailed Hawk. Adult groundhogs (5–10 lbs) represent high-value prey; hawks hunt them from elevated perches overlooking meadows and field edges, diving onto young or distracted individuals.
Red Fox hunts Groundhog in open fields and edge habitats, especially targeting young-of-year in June–July when juvenile groundhogs first emerge and have not yet learned to watch for predators.
Red Fox opportunistically preys on Eastern Gray Squirrel, particularly juveniles and ground-foraging adults in fall during acorn season.
Eastern Coyote occasionally preys on Virginia Opossum, particularly in winter when opossums are sluggish and vulnerable. Opossum's thanatosis response is ineffective against coyote.
Red Fox takes Virginia Opossum opportunistically, especially juveniles in summer.

Raccoon and Virginia Opossum share broadly overlapping diets and den sites, resulting in competition for carrion, fruit, invertebrates, and shelter. Raccoon is generally dominant in direct encounters.

Virginia Opossum regularly uses abandoned Groundhog burrows for temporary shelter and overwintering. Groundhog burrow systems provide critical below-frost-line refugia for opossums during winter cold snaps.
Red Fox frequently enlarges and uses abandoned Groundhog burrows as natal dens for raising kits in spring.

Black Bear uses dense hemlock groves for resting and day-bedding sites in summer, taking advantage of the cool shade. Hemlock stands also provide concealment during the sensitive denning search period in late fall.

Pileated Woodpecker forages on dead and dying hemlock trunks for carpenter ants and bark beetles, particularly in stands affected by Hemlock Woolly Adelgid. Hemlock decline paradoxically provides short-term foraging substrate for the woodpecker.
Red-tailed Hawk nests in the tall crown forks of mature hemlock trees in NE PA, using the dense evergreen branches for nest concealment and structural support.

Baltimore Oriole is one of the few songbirds that consistently eats hairy caterpillars — including Eastern Tent Caterpillar — that most birds avoid due to defensive hairs and chemical secretions. Orioles beat caterpillars against branches to remove the hairs before swallowing. During spring tent caterpillar outbreaks, orioles can significantly reduce colony size.

Orchard Oriole also takes tent caterpillars opportunistically during spring outbreaks, using the same dehusking technique as Baltimore Oriole. Less frequent than Baltimore Oriole due to later arrival timing.

Baltimore Oriole is a secondary pollinator of Eastern Columbine — probes the elongated nectar spurs and transfers pollen on its forehead and bill. Orioles supplement Ruby-throated Hummingbird as columbine pollinators in forest edge habitat.

Orchard Oriole is a frequent nectarivore at Eastern Columbine, probing tubular flowers and transferring pollen. More consistently nectarivorous than Baltimore Oriole, making it an important secondary pollinator of columbine and other native tubular-flowered plants.

Baltimore Oriole preferentially hangs its distinctive pendant nest from the flexible branch tips of Black Willow overhanging water — the pendulous branches provide structural support for the woven nest pouch while the water below deters mammalian predators. Willows along NE PA riparian corridors are among the most-used nesting trees for this species.

Baltimore Oriole eats ripe Black Cherry fruits heavily in July and August, coinciding with peak fruit ripening before southbound migration. Orioles are among the most efficient cherry consumers in NE PA's forest edge, consuming whole cherries and dispersing seeds via defecation across the landscape.

Bear Corn (Conopholis americana) is an obligate holoparasite of oak roots — it cannot survive without a host Quercus. The underground tuber-like attachment mass penetrates oak lateral roots and extracts water and carbohydrates entirely. White Oak is the preferred host in NE PA; Northern Red Oak is frequently used where White Oak is less abundant. A single host oak may support multiple bear corn clusters for decades.

American Black Bear is the primary consumer of Bear Corn in NE PA. Bears emerging from dens in late March–April actively seek out bear corn patches, eating entire above-ground clusters before spring berries and mast become available. Bear corn is calorie-dense relative to other early spring foods and its predictable, year-to-year emergence at fixed locations makes it a reliable post-denning resource. The plant's common name reflects this dependence.

Virginia Opossum is a significant consumer and seed disperser of Black Cherry fruit in late summer, eating fallen and low-hanging fruit and defecating seeds across home range. Opossums' broad foraging range and tolerance of secondary habitats (forest edge, suburban woodland) makes them effective dispersers of cherry seeds into fragmented landscapes.

Virginia Opossum and Raccoon extensively overlap in habitat use, nocturnal activity timing, diet (fruit, carrion, invertebrates, bird eggs), and den site selection (tree cavities, groundhog burrows). Where both are abundant, they compete for the same fallen fruit patches, carrion resources, and cavity dens. Raccoon is dominant in direct interactions; opossum avoids confrontation using thanatosis (playing dead) rather than aggression.

Little Brown Bat is a major predator of large night-flying moths including sphinx moths (Sphingidae). Sphinx moths fly at dusk and night, making them prime targets during the bat's peak foraging window. Bats detect moths by echolocation and pursue them in agile aerial pursuit — sphinx moths often perform evasive dives in response to ultrasonic pulses.

Big Brown Bat specializes in hard-bodied beetles more than any other NE PA bat species. Bark beetles emerging from trees at dusk are detected by echolocation and captured in aerial pursuit. A single Big Brown Bat colony can consume millions of beetles per season, providing meaningful suppression of bark beetle populations in stressed or declining forest stands.

Little Brown Bat uses large tree cavities excavated by Pileated Woodpecker as summer day roosts and maternity colony sites. Pileated cavities in large-diameter snags and live trees provide the warm, sheltered void geometry that bats require — particularly for maternity colonies where females need stable warm microclimate to rear pups. This commensalism makes Pileated Woodpecker indirectly important to bat population persistence.

Little Brown Bat and Big Brown Bat compete for the same roost sites (building attics, tree cavities, mine entrances) and overlap in aerial insect prey. Big Brown Bats are dominant at shared roost sites and can displace Little Brown Bat maternity colonies. With White-nose Syndrome decimating Little Brown Bat populations, Big Brown Bats are increasingly filling the nighttime insectivore niche across NE PA.

New England Aster is one of the most critical late-season resources for native bumble bees. Queens foraging in September–October are building fat reserves and carbohydrate stores before founding next year's colony. Aster fields can support dozens of bumble bee species simultaneously. The bee-aster relationship is so synchronized that bumble bee colony survival rates in fall correlate with local aster patch availability.

New England Aster is a critical fall migration nectar source for Monarch Butterfly. Monarchs passing through NE PA in September and October nectar heavily on asters alongside goldenrod — building the fat reserves needed for the 3,000-mile journey to Mexico. Asters extend the refueling window beyond goldenrod, which peaks in August–September.

Eastern Tiger Swallowtail adults nectar on New England Aster in fall while also transferring pollen — an effective mutual pollinator visit. Asters are among the top late-season nectar sources for swallowtails in NE PA.

Ruby-throated Hummingbird probes the tubular white flowers of Foxglove Beardtongue for nectar in May–June, transferring pollen on bill and forehead. Beardtongue's early summer bloom aligns with peak hummingbird breeding season in NE PA, making it a reliable nectar source during the period of highest hummingbird energy demand.

Foxglove Beardtongue is a bumble bee specialist: the tubular flower with a hairy lower lip is sized for large bees that must force entry, contacting both anthers and stigma. Bumble bees are the primary and most effective pollinators. The 'buzz pollination' of bees vibrating at specific frequencies releases pollen from anthers — a mutualism the plant depends on for seed set.

Pin Cherry is among the primary PA hosts for Eastern Tent Caterpillar in early successional habitat. The characteristic silk tents appear in Pin Cherry branch forks in April–May and can defoliate entire trees during peak outbreak years. Because Pin Cherry dominates disturbed patches, tent caterpillar outbreaks are especially conspicuous in recently cleared areas.

Cedar Waxwing flocks descend on Pin Cherry trees when fruits ripen in July–August and can strip an entire tree in a single visit. Waxwings pass cherry seeds through the gut intact, dispersing them into new disturbance patches — amplifying Pin Cherry's role as the first tree into clear-cuts and burned areas.

Pin Cherry is one of the earliest-blooming native trees in NE PA (April–May), providing critical pollen and nectar to newly emerged bumble bee queens before most other plants open. A single Pin Cherry in full bloom can host dozens of foraging queens simultaneously. This early bloom timing makes Pin Cherry disproportionately important to early-season native bee survival.

Chokecherry is a major PA host for Eastern Tent Caterpillar alongside Black Cherry and Pin Cherry. Tent caterpillar outbreaks in chokecherry thickets are common in NE PA riparian shrublands, where chokecherry often grows in dense stands that support large caterpillar populations.

Chokecherry is a top-tier Cedar Waxwing food source — the dense, abundant fruit clusters are efficiently harvested by flocks. Waxwings disperse chokecherry seeds widely. Astringency from cyanogenic compounds that deter mammals actually attracts birds, which tolerate the compounds better.

Black Bear is a major consumer of Chokecherry fruit in August–September. Bears break branches and strip clusters, tolerating the astringent compounds. Chokecherry provides high-carbohydrate fuel during fall hyperphagia when bears are building fat for winter denning.

Chokecherry blooms in May with dense white flower racemes that are heavily visited by bumble bees for nectar and pollen. Bumble bees are primary pollinators. The abundant early-season bloom makes chokecherry thickets important foraging habitat for native bees.

relations.notes: Three-way ecological interaction: Beech tree hosts aphids; ants protect aphids (mutualism); Harvester butterfly larvae prey on ants, disrupting the ant-aphid relationship and controlling aphid populations.
Both members of Sphingidae (sphinx/hawk moth) family. Similar size, nocturnal behavior, and flight characteristics. Important pollinators despite herbivorous larvae.
Both members of Hirundinidae (swallow) family. Share similar aerial insectivore ecology, migratory behavior, and insect-dependent diet despite different nesting substrate preferences.

PA's large mammal predator-prey system. Black Bear and Eastern Coyote are the primary remaining top predators; both limit Deer and Elk populations through fawn/calf predation. This trophic relationship partially compensates for the extirpation of Gray Wolf and Mountain Lion from PA — though deer populations remain far higher than under the full ancestral predator guild. Elk reintroduction adds a second large herbivore that creates browse competition with deer and additional prey for bears and coyotes.

NE PA mesofauna sharing abandoned burrow infrastructure. Groundhog excavates the burrows; abandoned systems are reused by Red Fox (natal dens), Raccoon (winter shelter), and Virginia Opossum (winter refuge). This secondary use network means Groundhog burrow density has outsized effects on local mesopredator populations beyond its direct role as prey.

Both members of Icteridae (blackbird) family, genus Icterus. Share pendulous woven nest construction, neotropical migration, and diet spanning insects, nectar, and fruit. Baltimore Oriole is the larger and more common of the two in NE PA; Orchard Oriole has the briefest summer residency of any NE PA breeder, departing in late July.

Both members of Order Chiroptera (bats), the only mammals capable of powered flight. Share echolocation, nocturnal insectivory, colonial maternity roosting, and cave/mine hibernation. Differ in size, prey specialization (moths vs. beetles), and White-nose Syndrome susceptibility — Big Brown Bat's greater WNS resistance is reshaping the relative abundance of the two species across NE PA.

Three native NE PA members of genus Prunus (family Rosaceae). Collectively support 456+ Lepidoptera species, are the primary Eastern Tent Caterpillar hosts, and produce fruit that fuels Cedar Waxwing, American Robin, thrush, and Black Bear fattening cycles. Black Cherry is the large forest tree; Pin Cherry is the disturbance pioneer; Chokecherry is the riparian-edge shrub. Together they provide Prunus resources across every successional stage.
